en.extract:
The Nazca booby (Sula granti) is a large seabird of the booby family, Sulidae, native to the eastern Pacific. First described by Walter Rothschild in 1902, it was long considered a subspecies of the masked booby until recognised as distinct genetically and behaviorally in 2002. It has a typical sulid body shape, with a long pointed orange-yellow bill, long neck, aerodynamic body, long slender wings and pointed tail.  The adult is bright white with black and white wings, a black tail and a dark face mask.


Taxonomy
Walter Rothschild organised and sent an expedition to the Galapagos Islands in 1897 to collect and review the animal life there. He wrote of a distinctive booby there, which he and William Robert Ogilvie-Grant diagnosed as the Peruvian booby (Sula variegata), then only known from juvenile plumage. Later, in 1902, Rothschild named it a new species, Sula granti. Rothschild later reclassified as a subspecies of the masked booby.
In 1998, Pitman and colleagues observed that Nazca boobies on Clipperton Island did not interbreed with masked boobies there.
The genus Sula was previously placed in the order Pelecaniformes, but recently was collected in the family Sulidae and order Suliformes, together with 8 other genera. The Nazca booby was considered conspecific with the masked booby but was reassigned to a separate species based on mitochondrial DNA analyses. It is likely to have diverged 400,000-500,000 years ago.


Description
The species has a yellow iris, orange and pinkish beak, black facial skin in the form of a mask, and grey feet. Adults present white plumage with black tips of the wings and tail. The female is bigger and heavier than the male, has a slightly differently colored beak, and squawks while the male whistles. Chicks are snow white and fluffy, plumage changing to grey along with beak and feet upon fledging.


Distribution and habitat
The species occurs in the eastern Pacific from the islands in Baja California to the Galápagos Islands and the Isla de la Plata in Ecuador and Malpelo in Colombia. Individuals have been recorded as vagrants in the western Pacific, including near Waigeo in Indonesia, and off north-western Australia.


Ecology


Feeding
The Nazca booby preys on small fish caught by diving at high speed from flight into the ocean. The main food species is South American pilchard, but also take flying fish, anchovies and squid, especially during the El Niño events, when sardine numbers are low. Because of their sexual dimorphism, females tend to feed on bigger prey and dive deeper.


Reproduction
The Nazca booby nests near cliffs on bare ground with little to no vegetation. The male chooses and defends a territory, then enters into courtship to attract females.
Like many seabirds, the species has a long lifespan combined with low annual reproduction and long periods of development in the young. Clutch size is one or two eggs, due to the low hatching success, however when 2 eggs are laid and they both hatch, it is common for only one of the chicks to survive. Usually, whichever is the first to hatch is the one that survives.
While many species of birds regulate egg temperature via an incubation patch, a layer of bare skin that allows birds to transmit heat into their eggs, the Suliformes instead use the skin on their webbed feet in addition to heat transferred from the breast. The feet are heavily vascularized, especially during the nesting period. Both the male and the female show parental care. Usually the chick that hatches first is bigger and becomes aggressive towards its sibling, excluding it from feeding and eventually starving it.
The energy investment on the parent's part is very high, so their metabolic rates change during the nesting season. This causes both parents to lose similar amounts of body weight and suffer a decline in their immune system activity. This adjustment does not take place when the parents decide not to nest, a decision that is mostly driven by food availability, which in turn depends on ocean current and climate patterns such as those driven by the El Niño oscillation.
Siblicide has been well studied in this species; the first chick is born around five days before the second and is larger and stronger by the time the second is born. It drags its younger sibling out of the nest. Field experiments in the Galapagos demonstrated that the boobies can manage to feed two chicks without too much difficulty. This raises questions as to the origin of the phenomenon.


Parasites
The vampire ground finch sometimes feeds on the blood of the Nazca booby.


Conservation
The Nazca booby is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. Although populations are thought to decrease to some extent, this decline is not strong enough to require classification in a threat category. Some of the factors that influence the decrease of populations are overfishing and marine pollution.


Gallery


References

The '''Nazca booby''' ('''''Sula granti''''') is a large [[seabird]] of the [[booby]] family, [[Sulidae]], native to the eastern Pacific. First described by [[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Walter Rothschild]] in 1902, it was long considered a subspecies of the [[masked booby]] until recognised as distinct genetically and behaviorally in 2002. It has a typical sulid body shape, with a long pointed orange-yellow bill, long neck, aerodynamic body, long slender wings and pointed tail.  The adult is bright white with black and white wings, a black tail and a dark face mask.

==Taxonomy==
[[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Walter Rothschild]] organised and sent an expedition to the Galapagos Islands in 1897 to collect and review the animal life there. He wrote of a distinctive booby there, which he and [[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant]] diagnosed as the [[Peruvian booby]] (''Sula variegata''), then only known from juvenile plumage.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Rothschild | first1= Walter | first2= E. |last2=Hartert | year=1899| title= A Review of the Ornithology of the Galapagos Islands. With Notes on the Webster-Harris Expedition | journal =Novitates Zoologicae | volume= VI | issue= 2 |  pages= 85–205 [178] | url =https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/3258923 }}</ref> Later, in 1902, Rothschild named it a new species, ''Sula granti''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rothschild |first1=Walter |title=An overlooked species of gannet. |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=1902 |volume=13 |page=7 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/37116689}}</ref> Rothschild later reclassified as a subspecies of the [[masked booby]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rothschild |first1=Walter |title=Notes on the genus ''Sula'' |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=1915 |volume=35 |pages=41–45 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32439492}}</ref>

In 1998, Pitman and colleagues observed that Nazca boobies on [[Clipperton Island]] did not interbreed with masked boobies there.<ref name=pitman1998>{{Cite journal|last1=Pitman|first1=Robert L.|last2=Jehl|first2=Joseph R.|last3=Joseph L. Jehl|date=1998-01-01|title=Geographic Variation and Reassessment of Species Limits in the "Masked" Boobies of the Eastern Pacific Ocean|jstor=4163925|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=110|issue=2|pages=155–70}}</ref>

The genus ''[[Sula (bird)|Sula]]'' was previously placed in the order [[Pelecaniformes]], but recently was collected in the family [[Sulidae]] and order [[Suliformes]], together with 8 other genera.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Wenny|first1=Daniel G.|last2=Devault|first2=Travis L.|last3=Johnson|first3=Matthew D.|last4=Kelly|first4=Dave|last5=Sekercioglu|first5=Cagan H.|last6=Tomback|first6=Diana F.|author6-link=Diana Tomback|last7=Whelan|first7=Christopher J.|date=2011-01-01|title=The Need to Quantify Ecosystem Services Provided By Birds|journal=The Auk|volume=128|issue=1|pages=1–14|doi=10.1525/auk.2011.10248|s2cid=4787044|issn=0004-8038|url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/icwdm_usdanwrc/1347|doi-access=free}}</ref> The Nazca booby was considered conspecific with the [[masked booby]] but was reassigned to a separate species based on [[mitochondrial DNA]] analyses. It is likely to have diverged 400,000-500,000 years ago.<ref name="Friesen 820–826"/>

==Description==
The species has a yellow iris, orange and pinkish beak, black facial skin in the form of a mask, and grey feet. Adults present white plumage with black tips of the wings and tail. The female is bigger and heavier than the male, has a slightly differently colored beak, and squawks while the male whistles. Chicks are snow white and fluffy, plumage changing to grey along with beak and feet upon fledging.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Ridgely|first1=R. S.|first2=P.J.|last2=Greenfield|year=2006|title=Aves del Ecuador. Guía de Campo|publisher=Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco|page=92}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The species occurs in the eastern Pacific from the islands in Baja California to the [[Galápagos Islands]] and the [[Isla de la Plata]] in Ecuador and [[Malpelo Island|Malpelo]] in Colombia.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/factsheet/22728990|title=Nazca Booby (Sula granti) - BirdLife species factsheet|website=www.birdlife.org|access-date=2016-10-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Pitman|first1=Robert L.|last2=Jehl|first2=Joseph R.|last3=Joseph L. Jehl|date=1998-01-01|title=Geographic Variation and Reassessment of Species Limits in the "Masked" Boobies of the Eastern Pacific Ocean|jstor=4163925|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=110|issue=2|pages=155–170}}</ref> Individuals have been recorded as vagrants in the western Pacific, including near [[Waigeo]] in Indonesia,<ref>{{Cite web |title=First Nazca Booby for Indonesia |url=https://www.ornis-birding.com/news/first-nazca-booby-for-indonesia |access-date=2025-03-18 |website=Ornis Birding |language=en}}</ref> and off north-western Australia.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-03-11 |title=BirdLife Australia Rarities Committee - Birdata |url=https://birdata.birdlife.org.au/birdlife-australia-rarities-committee |access-date=2025-03-18 |language=en-US}}</ref>

==Ecology==

===Feeding===
The Nazca booby preys on small fish caught by diving at high speed from flight into the ocean. The main food species is [[South American pilchard]], but also take flying fish, anchovies and squid, especially during the [[El Niño]] events, when sardine numbers are low.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Crawford|first1=R. J. M.|last2=Goya|first2=E.|last3=Roux|first3=J.-P.|last4=Zavalaga|first4=C. B.|date=2006-11-01|title=Comparison of assemblages and some life-history traits of seabirds in the Humboldt and Benguela systems|journal=African Journal of Marine Science|volume=28|issue=3–4|pages=553–560|doi=10.2989/18142320609504205|bibcode=2006AfJMS..28..553C |s2cid=84133059|issn=1814-232X}}</ref> Because of their sexual dimorphism, females tend to feed on bigger prey and dive deeper.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=García-R|first1=Silvana|last2=López-Victoria|first2=Mateo|date=2008-12-15|title=Sexual differences in body size and diet in the Nazca Booby (Sula granti)|journal=Ornitología Colombiana|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/238717017|issue=7|issn=1794-0915}}</ref>

===Reproduction===
The Nazca booby nests near cliffs on bare ground with little to no vegetation.<ref name="Friesen 820–826">{{Cite journal|last1=Friesen|first1=V. L.|last2=Anderson|first2=D. J.|last3=Steeves|first3=T. E.|last4=Jones|first4=H.|last5=Schreiber|first5=E. A.|date=2002-01-01|title=Molecular Support for Species Status of the Nazca Booby (Sula granti)|jstor=4089981|journal=The Auk|volume=119|issue=3|pages=820–826}}</ref> The male chooses and defends a territory, then enters into courtship to attract females.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Maness|first1=Terri J.|last2=Westbrock|first2=Mark A.|last3=Anderson|first3=David J.|date=2007-01-01|title=Ontogenic Sex Ratio Variation in Nazca Boobies Ends in Male-Biased Adult Sex Ratio|jstor=4501789|journal=Waterbirds: The International Journal of Waterbird Biology|volume=30|issue=1|pages=10–16|doi=10.1675/1524-4695(2007)030[0010:osrvin]2.0.co;2|s2cid=44437249 }}</ref>

Like many seabirds, the species has a long lifespan combined with low annual reproduction and long periods of development in the young. Clutch size is one or two eggs, due to the low hatching success, however when 2 eggs are laid and they both hatch, it is common for only one of the chicks to survive.<ref name=tarlow/> Usually, whichever is the first to hatch is the one that survives.<ref>{{cite news|last=Greenfieldboyce|first=Nell|title=For birds, siblinghood can be a matter of life or death|url=https://www.npr.org/2024/05/01/1248050363/bird-siblings-siblingcide-boobies-woodpeckers-life-death|date=May 1, 2024|website=[[NPR]]}}</ref>

While many species of birds regulate egg temperature via an incubation patch, a layer of bare skin that allows birds to transmit heat into their eggs, the Suliformes instead use the skin on their webbed feet in addition to heat transferred from the breast. The feet are heavily [[Angiogenesis|vascularized]], especially during the nesting period.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Morgan|first1=Stephanie M.|last2=Ashley-Ross|first2=Miriam A.|last3=Anderson|first3=David J.|date=2003-05-01|title=Foot-Mediated Incubation: Nazca Booby (Sula granti) Feet as Surrogate Brood Patches|journal=Physiological and Biochemical Zoology|volume=76|issue=3|pages=360–366|doi=10.1086/375430|pmid=12905122|s2cid=2988234|issn=1522-2152}}</ref> Both the male and the female show parental care.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Maness|first1=Terri J.|last2=Anderson|first2=David J.|date=2007-01-01|title=Serial Monogamy and Sex Ratio Bias in Nazca Boobies|jstor=25249287|journal=Proceedings: Biological Sciences|volume=274|issue=1621|pages=2047–2054|doi=10.1098/rspb.2007.0578|pmid=17567557|pmc=2275186}}</ref> Usually the chick that hatches first is bigger and becomes aggressive towards its sibling, excluding it from feeding and eventually starving it.<ref name=tarlow>{{Cite journal|last1=Tarlow|first1=Elisa M|last2=Wikelski|first2=Martin|last3=Anderson|first3=David J|date=2001-08-01|title=Hormonal Correlates of Siblicide in Galapagos Nazca Boobies|journal=Hormones and Behavior|volume=40|issue=1|pages=14–20|doi=10.1006/hbeh.2001.1661|pmid=11467880|s2cid=24718563}}</ref>

The energy investment on the parent's part is very high, so their metabolic rates change during the nesting season. This causes both parents to lose similar amounts of body weight and suffer a decline in their immune system activity.<ref name=":1" /> This adjustment does not take place when the parents decide not to nest, a decision that is mostly driven by food availability, which in turn depends on ocean current and climate patterns such as those driven by the [[El Niño]] oscillation.<ref name=":0" />

[[Siblicide]] has been well studied in this species; the first chick is born around five days before the second and is larger and stronger by the time the second is born. It drags its younger sibling out of the nest. Field experiments in the Galapagos demonstrated that the boobies can manage to feed two chicks without too much difficulty. This raises questions as to the origin of the phenomenon.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Anderson | first= David J. | year=1990 | title=Evaluation of Obligate Siblicide in Boobies. 2: Food Limitation and Parent-Offspring Conflict | journal=Evolution | volume=44| issue= 8 | pages= 2069–82 | jstor=2409616| doi= 10.1111/j.1558-5646.1990.tb04312.x | pmid= 28564435 | s2cid= 205777351 }}</ref><ref>Anderson, David J. 1990. "Evolution of Obligate Siblicide in Boobies 1. A Test of the Insurance-Egg Hypothesis." The American Naturalist 135, vol. 3: 334-350</ref>

===Parasites===
The [[vampire ground finch]] sometimes feeds on the blood of the Nazca booby.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Michel|first1=Alice J.|last2=Ward|first2=Lewis M.|last3=Goffredi|first3=Shana K.|last4=Dawson|first4=Katherine S.|last5=Baldassarre|first5=Daniel T.|last6=Brenner|first6=Alec|last7=Gotanda|first7=Kiyoko M.|last8=McCormack|first8=John E.|last9=Mullin|first9=Sean W.|date=2018-09-19|title=The gut of the finch: uniqueness of the gut microbiome of the Galápagos vampire finch|journal=Microbiome|volume=6|issue=1|page=167|doi=10.1186/s40168-018-0555-8|issn=2049-2618|pmc=6146768|pmid=30231937 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2018Micb....6..167M }}</ref>

==Conservation==
The Nazca booby is classified as [[Least Concern]] by the [[IUCN]]. Although populations are thought to decrease to some extent, this decline is not strong enough to require classification in a threat category. Some of the factors that influence the decrease of populations are [[overfishing]] and [[marine pollution]].<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Takasuka|first1=Akinori|last2=Oozeki|first2=Yoshioki|last3=Aoki|first3=Ichiro|date=2007-05-01|title=Optimal growth temperature hypothesis: Why do anchovy flourish and sardine collapse or vice versa under the same ocean regime?|journal=Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences|volume=64|issue=5|pages=768–776|doi=10.1139/f07-052|bibcode=2007CJFAS..64..768T |issn=0706-652X}}</ref>

zh.extract:
橙嘴鲣鸟（學名：Sula granti，台湾作納茲卡鰹鳥）为鰹鳥科鲣鸟属的一种。曾被认为是蓝脸鲣鸟的一个亚种。分布于太平洋东海岸。通体羽毛除了飞羽和尾羽外均为白色，眼部周围为蓝黑色。喙长，为橙色。
